# Data and Code Practices {#sec-data-code}

Reproducibility is a core value of the lab (see @sec-values). This chapter lays out the standards we follow for data management, version control, and code quality.

## Version control with Git and GitHub

All analysis code lives in Git repositories hosted on the lab's GitHub organization. The basics:

- **Commit often** with clear, descriptive messages. A good commit message explains *why*, not just *what*.
- **Use branches** for new features or analyses. Merge into `main` via pull request when the work is ready.
- **Never commit data** to a Git repository. Use `.gitignore` to exclude data files, outputs, and large binaries. If you need to track a large file, use [Git LFS](https://git-lfs.com/).
- **Never commit credentials**, API keys, or passwords. If you accidentally do, rotate the credential immediately.

### Repository structure

We do not enforce a single template, but a typical project repository looks something like:

```
project-name/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── data/               # Symlink to Savio project storage (not tracked)
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_clean.R
│   ├── 02_estimate.R
│   └── 03_figures.R
├── output/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/               # Notes, methods documentation
└── slurm/              # Job submission scripts
```

### Code review

For larger or shared projects, use pull requests and ask a lab mate to review your code before merging. Code review is not about finding fault — it is about catching bugs, improving clarity, and sharing knowledge.

## Coding standards

### R

R is our primary language for econometric and geospatial analysis. Conventions:

- Use the [tidyverse style guide](https://style.tidyverse.org/) as a baseline.
- Prefer `data.table` for performance-critical operations on large datasets.
- Use `here::here()` for file paths — never hardcode absolute paths in scripts.
- Use `library()` calls at the top of every script, not scattered throughout.
- Name scripts with a numbered prefix that reflects execution order (e.g., `B01_Predict_Prevalence.R`).

### Python

For machine learning, satellite image processing, and some data engineering tasks:

- Follow [PEP 8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/).
- Use virtual environments or conda environments — never install into the system Python.
- Pin dependencies in a `requirements.txt` or `environment.yml`.

### Stata

Some projects (particularly replications or collaborations with economists) use Stata:

- Use `.do` files with clear headers describing purpose, inputs, and outputs.
- Set paths at the top of the master `.do` file using globals.
- Store logs for all estimation runs.

## Data management

### Principles

- **Raw data is sacred.** Never modify raw data files. All transformations should happen in code, producing processed outputs in a separate directory.
- **Document everything.** Every dataset should have a README describing its source, collection date, processing steps, file formats, and naming conventions.
- **Deduplicate.** Before downloading a dataset to Savio, check the **#computing** Slack channel to see if someone already has it (see @sec-project-storage).

### File naming

Use consistent, descriptive file names. Avoid spaces and special characters. Good patterns:

- `era5_daily_temperature_2000_2020.nc`
- `pfpr_africa_2x2_degree.feather`
- `bootstrap_results_iter_0001.feather`

### Sensitive data

Some projects involve data covered by data use agreements (DUAs) or institutional review board (IRB) protocols. If you are working with sensitive data:

- Store it only in approved locations (see @sec-project-storage for secure storage options on Savio).
- Do not share it on Slack, email, or Google Drive unless explicitly permitted.
- Follow the terms of the DUA exactly — when in doubt, ask Tamma.

## Reproducibility checklist

Before sharing results or submitting a paper, verify:

- [ ] The full pipeline runs end-to-end from raw data to final outputs.
- [ ] All dependencies (R packages, Python libraries, system tools) are documented with versions.
- [ ] Random seeds are set for any stochastic processes.
- [ ] File paths use `here()` or equivalent — no hardcoded absolute paths.
- [ ] A README explains how to reproduce the results.
- [ ] Intermediate files can be regenerated from code (they do not need to be archived).
